displayTitle: Passing the Course Doesn't Certify You

beats.narration: Finishing a course proves nothing official. Under the employer-based model, certification happens only when exams pass, vision is current, and the authority signs.

payload.title: WHO ACTUALLY CERTIFIES YOU
payload.sub: The employer signs — after exams, vision, and grades.

beats.narration: Level One and Two face three exams. General tests the method's physics, closed book. Specific covers your shop's procedures. Practical is hands-on, graded on a checklist.
Grading is a composite, commonly eighty percent. Because an average hides weak links, practices add a floor — seventy on each exam. Vision gates everything.
Then the employer certifies — written testimony backed by records: level, method, exam grades, vision dates, signature. Because the employer signs, it doesn't transfer jobs.

payload.heading: Certification is the record behind it
payload.note: LEVEL · METHOD · GRADES · VISION · SIGNATURE — VALID ONLY HERE

beats.narration: Keep the lifecycle as one picture, and every exam variation falls into place.

payload.sub: Interrupted service, termination, reinstatement — all side paths off this one loop.
